es.text: Costar un ojo de la cara
es.literal: to cost an eye from the face
es.example: Me encantaron esos zapatos, pero costaban un ojo de la cara.
es.exampleTranslation: I loved those shoes, but they cost a fortune.

it.literal: to cost an eye of the head
it.example: Quel ristorante era buonissimo, ma ci è costato un occhio della testa.
it.exampleTranslation: That restaurant was excellent, but it cost us a fortune.

bridge.note: ojo / occhio are direct cognates from Latin oculus. Spanish takes the eye from the face; Italian from the head.

interference.es_to_it: ci è costato — costare takes essere in the passato prossimo, not avere.

review.notes: Cue drafted by Claude 2026-07-20 from the entry's meaning and bridge — English situational framing, not a language claim, so it needs editorial taste rather than native review. Override freely.

hook.headline: It costs an eye in both languages. The verb is the trap.
hook.subhead: Costare takes essere, not avere. Spanish gives you no warning.

cue: How do you complain that something cost a fortune?
